## 8. Conclusion

This research introduced IslandPilot, a pipeline architecture that uses market regimes as the structuring principle for island-model evolutionary computation. The system makes three specific design decisions that distinguish it from prior evolutionary trading system work: (1) island topology is derived from the data via hierarchical GMM regime discovery, not specified a priori; (2) each island evolves a per-regime execution policy spanning sizing, grid, take-profit, and pipeline-level risk-bounding parameters — sufficient to convert a catastrophic Martingale into a near-breakeven one without evolving the entry signal — with an explicit design endpoint for the wider 7-group genome identified as the next direction for the conference-paper extension; (3) all fitness evaluations run on the production execution engine with real per-candle OANDA bid-ask spread, swap, and slippage costs, not on simplified simulators.

The empirical result supports a specific claim and rejects another. On a 15.5-month strictly out-of-sample evaluation (January 2025 to mid-April 2026, EUR-USD 5m), IslandPilot reduces net loss from -87.38% to -0.83% and collapses maximum drawdown from 84.73% to 0.75% (≈113× smaller); profit factor rises from 0.717 to 0.877 while remaining sub-unity. The improvement operates through three risk-bounding mechanisms — session-volume collapse, position-size compression, depth-distribution shift — that compound multiplicatively on the loss path (full mechanism analysis in §6.6; framing in §7.1). It is not entry-quality engineering: regime-structured evolution under this instrument and these costs does not manufacture directional alpha where none exists in the underlying random-entry signal. It does bound the loss envelope tightly enough to convert a catastrophic strategy into a near-breakeven one.

The principal contribution of this research is twofold. First, regime-structured evolutionary optimisation can be used as a capital-preservation mechanism for Martingale-family strategies under realistic execution costs, collapsing the drawdown envelope by roughly two orders of magnitude without requiring directional alpha (training cost: 12,600 real-engine backtests in ~10.5 h on a Google Cloud c2-standard-60; details §6.2). Second, the honest accounting of what the pipeline does and does not do (capital preservation succeeds; alpha generation does not) isolates the structural limits of the approach and provides a falsifiable baseline for future work on the same class of strategies.

Two methodological contributions accompany this result. First, evolutionary fitness evaluation must be conducted on the production execution engine: parameters evolved on simplified simulators without transaction costs fail to transfer. Second, the engine-evolver interface itself requires explicit correctness conditions that are not apparent from simpler simulators: categorical genes must be resolved to strategy-semantic values before evaluation, and engine state must be reset between trading cycles under every close path including margin-driven force-liquidation. Both conditions produce statistically degenerate fitness signals when violated, making them easy to misread as insufficient evolutionary budget rather than correctness failures at the engine boundary. These conditions are documented in Section 4.2 to support reproducibility.

### 8.1 Future Directions

The current system was developed and reported in two iterations. Iteration 1, the cloud-trained 20-gene 3-group model, produces the §6 results — and crucially, achieves the headline 113-fold drawdown collapse without evolving any entry-signal gene. Iteration 1's evidence motivates the research questions that the conference-paper extension this work is heading towards is designed to investigate. Several directions are identified; each represents a substantial engineering and research effort, and each is deliberately scoped beyond the dissertation timeframe.

**Iteration 2 full-scale evaluation.** The corrected pipeline (7 tunable groups, 57 genes per genome, categorical-gene resolver, NaN-safe fitness composite) is implementation-complete and architecturally validated under the Section 6.7 pre-flight protocol. Its full-scale evaluation on the 2022–2024 training window with production-equivalent generation count and population size is the most direct extension of this work — and the most strongly motivated by Iteration 1's evidence. The open empirical questions are: whether evolving entry signal and direction bias per regime produces a directional edge that survives the full 2025-2026 OOS window (Iteration 1's L0 win rate of 5.6% suggests this is non-trivial); whether the wider search space risks overfitting at the regime-population scale; and whether the additional fitness-shape modifications (Section 4) materially change the GA's reachable set. Iteration 2's full-scale execution is deferred pending further heavy computation.

The current Iteration 1 system also operates on a single instrument (EUR-USD) and waits for regime-appropriate conditions to arise on that instrument. Several architectural extensions beyond Iteration 2 would transform IslandPilot from a single-instrument regime adapter into a multi-instrument allocation framework. Due to scope constraints, these directions were identified but not investigated in the dissertation.

**Multi-Instrument Momentum Scanner.** The most impactful extension is a cross-instrument screening layer that identifies, in real time, which instruments from a monitored universe are currently in regimes where the Martingale strategy is expected to perform well, then allocates capital accordingly. The scanner would maintain a trained IslandPilot regime tree per instrument, compute regime classifications continuously, and rank instruments by the expected profit factor of the best genome for the current regime. Capital would flow toward instruments in favourable regimes (high-confidence, historically profitable regime leaves) and away from instruments in hostile regimes (trending, high-bust-probability leaves). This converts the problem from "wait for EUR-USD to enter a good regime" to "find the instrument that is in a good regime right now." The architectural challenge is non-trivial: regime trees trained independently per instrument produce incomparable regime identifiers, so a shared macro-regime representation (a universal feature space with instrument-specific sub-clustering) would be required to enable cross-instrument ranking. The screening frequency, rebalancing cost model, and correlation structure between instrument regime states (whether good regimes on EUR-USD and GBP-USD co-occur or alternate) are open empirical questions that determine whether the multi-instrument portfolio achieves genuine diversification or merely replicates the same regime exposure across correlated pairs.

**Online Regime Tree Adaptation.** Addressing the frozen-tree limitation noted in §3.6 and §7.7, an online adaptation mechanism would detect novel regime states (via log-likelihood monitoring on the GMM) and spawn new regime leaves with freshly initialised island populations. The evolutionary challenge is cold-start: a new island has no fitness history and must evolve from random initialisation while the market is already in the regime that triggered its creation. Warm-starting from the nearest sibling island's best genome is a practical heuristic, but the theoretical convergence properties of this online island-creation process are unexplored. This extension would transform IslandPilot from a static classifier into a non-stationary adaptive system, a fundamentally harder problem with potentially much larger payoff.

**Hierarchical Temporal Decomposition.** The current system operates at a single timescale (5m candles). Financial markets exhibit structure at multiple temporal scales simultaneously: a 5m chart may show mean-reversion within a 1h trend within a daily range. A multi-scale IslandPilot would maintain separate regime trees at each timescale (5m, 1h, 4h, daily), with a meta-layer that combines regime classifications across scales to produce a composite regime state. The genome for each composite state would encode parameters appropriate for the dominant timescale dynamics. The HAR-RV multi-scale volatility features already in the 30-indicator pool (Section 3.1) provide the feature-space foundation; the architectural challenge is defining the composition operator that maps multiple per-scale regime classifications to a single composite state without combinatorial explosion (k scales × r regimes per scale = r^k composite states).

**Transfer Learning Across Instruments.** A regime tree pre-trained on EUR-USD captures general market microstructure patterns (volatility clustering, session-based periodicity, trend/range alternation) that partially transfer to other liquid FX pairs. The pre-trained island populations would serve as warm-start initialisation for evolution on a target instrument, reducing the 12,600-evaluation training cost to a fine-tuning budget of a few hundred evaluations. The empirical question is how much transfers: closely correlated pairs (EUR-USD and GBP-USD) likely share macro-regime structure, while structurally different instruments (gold, Bitcoin, equity indices) may share only the broadest volatility regime distinctions. Quantifying the transfer coefficient per instrument class would establish whether IslandPilot can be deployed as a general-purpose adaptive framework or whether it requires full retraining per instrument.

**Integration with Within-Cycle Tactical Decisions.** A complementary direction is to add a within-cycle abort mechanism that decides whether to terminate the current session early given the active market state, in contrast to the pipeline's between-session decision of which parameters to use given the current regime. A reinforcement-learning abort policy operating at within-cycle granularity (state: depth, accumulated drawdown, time-in-session, regime confidence; action: continue or terminate) would address risk at a finer temporal grain than the pipeline's per-cycle parameter selection. Because the two mechanisms address complementary aspects of risk management (within-cycle tactical decisions and between-cycle strategic configuration), they are architecturally compatible. The combined system would represent a two-level adaptive hierarchy: the pipeline selects the execution policy (strategic), and the within-cycle policy modifies execution within that policy (tactical). Whether the compounding effect is additive or multiplicative is an open question with significant practical implications.

**Validation work in progress for journal extension.** Several methodological extensions are in progress at the time of dissertation submission and will be integrated into the journal version: (i) walk-forward validation with rolling re-training across multiple non-overlapping OOS windows to test temporal generalisation beyond the single 2025-to-2026 window reported here; (ii) combinatorial purged cross-validation (CPCV) adapted to per-regime island populations, providing a stronger overfitting bound than the current hold-out protocol (Bailey et al. 2014; Arian et al. 2024); (iii) honest re-runs of the comparison systems (GTSBotPilot, FinRLPilot) under the same production execution engine on the canonical OOS window; (iv) sensitivity analyses to the methodological hyperparameters (hysteresis margin δ, MI threshold α, drawdown function constants, GA operator rates); (v) preliminary multi-instrument experiments on GBP-USD and USD-JPY to test transfer of the regime-structured island topology across currency pairs. The dissertation reports the current state under bounded research time; these extensions are deliberately deferred rather than rushed.

 -
